paper_title: From Pixels to Concepts: Do Segmentation Models Understand What They Segment?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Segmentation is a fundamental vision task underlying numerous downstream applications. Recent promptable segmentation models, such as Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), extend segmentation from category-agnostic mask prediction to concept-guided localization conditioned on high-level textual prompts. However, existing benchmarks primarily evaluate mask accuracy or object presence, leaving unclear whether these models faithfully ground the queried concept or instead rely on visually salient but semantically misleading cues. We introduce CAFE: \textbf{C}ounterfactual \textbf{A}ttribute \textbf{F}actuality \textbf{E}valuation, a novel benchmark for evaluating concept-faithful segmentation in promptable segmentation models. Our \textbf{CAFE} is built on attribute-level counterfactual manipulation: the target region and ground-truth mask are preserved, while attributes such as surface appearance, context, or material composition are modified to introduce misleading semantic cues. The benchmark contains 2,146 paired test samples, each consisting of a target image, a ground-truth mask, a positive prompt, and a misleading negative prompt. These samples cover three counterfactual categories: Superficial Mimicry (\textbf{SM}), Context Conflict (\textbf{CC}), and Ontological Conflict (\textbf{OC}). We evaluate various model types and sizes on our CAFE. Experiments reveal a systematic gap between localization quality and concept discrimination: models often generate accurate masks even for misleading prompts, suggesting that strong mask prediction does not necessarily imply faithful semantic grounding. Our CAFE provides a controlled benchmark for diagnosing whether promptable segmentation models perform concept-faithful grounding rather than shortcut-driven mask retrieval.

sections.content: From Pixels to Concepts: Do Segmentation Models Understand What They Segment? Shuang Liang1,3,, Zeqing Wang2,, Yuxian Li1,, Xihui Liu1, Han Wang1,3 1Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, The University of Hong Kong 2School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University 3CASIC, The University of Hong Kong 6 2 0 M 0 1 ] . [ 1 1 9 5 9 0 . 5 0 6 2 : r 1 Figure 1: CAFE focuses on attribute-level counterfactual evaluation, where specific visual attributes are modified within fixed target mask inherited from the original annotation. Positive prompts refer to the semantically valid concept for the edited target region, while negative prompts refer to misleading but semantically invalid concepts. The original object remains the positive concept in Superficial Mimicry and Context Conflict; for Ontological Conflict the converted material becomes the positive concept and the original object becomes the misleading negative concept. For example, after replacing the background of teddy bear with snowy scene, SAM3 correctly segments the object when prompted with the positive prompt teddy bear. However, when given the negative prompt polar bear, which is plausible under the surrounding visual context but semantically inconsistent with the object identity, SAM3 still segments the same instance with high confidence. Abstract. Segmentation is fundamental vision task underlying numerous downstream applications. Recent promptable segmentation models, such as Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), extend segmentation from category-agnostic mask prediction to concept-guided localization conditioned on high-level textual prompts. However, existing benchmarks primarily evaluate mask accuracy or object presence, leaving unclear whether these models faithfully ground the queried concept or instead rely on visually salient but semantically misleading cues. We introduce CAFE: Counterfactual Attribute Factuality Evaluation, novel benchmark for evaluating concept-faithful segmentation in promptable segmentation models. Our CAFE is built on attribute-level counterfactual manipulation: the target region and ground-truth mask are preserved, while attributes such as surface appearance, context, or material composition are modified to introduce misleading semantic cues. The benchmark contains 2,146 paired test samples, each consisting of target image, ground-truth mask, positive prompt, and misleading negative prompt. These samples cover three counterfactual categories: Superficial Mimicry (SM), Context Conflict (CC), and Ontological Conflict (OC). We evaluate various model types and sizes on our CAFE. Experiments reveal systematic gap between localization quality and concept discrimination: models often generate accurate masks even for misleading prompts, suggesting that strong mask prediction does not necessarily imply faithful semantic grounding. Our CAFE provides controlled benchmark for diagnosing whether promptable segmentation models perform concept-faithful grounding rather than shortcut-driven mask retrieval. Project Page: https://t-s-liang.github.io/CAFE Code: https://github.com/T-S-Liang/CAFE Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/teemosliang/CAFE 1. Introduction Segmentation has long been central problem in computer vision, evolving from category-level dense prediction in semantic segmentation [2, 34], to instance-aware mask prediction [3, 8, 23], and more recently to open-vocabulary and promptable segmentation [5, 26, 35, 37]. This progression relaxes closed-set categories and enables prompt-guided region association. Early promptable segmentation models, such as SAM [13] and SAM2 [25], focus on visual prompts, such as points, boxes and primarily address spatial grounding without explicit textual concept conditioning. In parallel, open-vocabulary segmentation and grounding-segmentation pipelines use language queries to localize semantic regions, often by coupling grounding or detection model, such as Grounding DINO [20], with mask generator [26]. Recently, SAM3 [1] introduced promptable concept segmentation (PCS), an end-to-end formulation that directly produces masks from concept prompts, without relying on an explicit grounding or detection stage to generate intermediate boxes. Standard benchmarks such as COCO [18], ADE20K [38], and LVIS [6] primarily evaluate segmentation accuracy over predefined visual categories. Recent counterfactual benchmarks, such as HalluSegBench [17] further tests object-level counterfactual hallucination by pairing factual images with counterfactual images in which the referred object is absent. However, counterfactual segmentation is not limited to object-level presence or absence. Fine-grained conflicts can arise when the target region remains visible and localizable, but attributes that affect concept identity, such as surface appearance, surrounding context, or material composition, are deliberately modified. In this setting, model may produce geometrically accurate mask for semantically invalid prompt. Existing benchmarks therefore provide limited Equal contribution. Corresponding author. Figure 2: Examples in our CAFE. Each sample contains counterfactually edited target image, ground-truth mask for the target region, positive prompt that is semantically valid for the target, and misleading negative prompt that is visually plausible but semantically invalid. The examples cover three attribute-level intervention types: Superficial Mimicry (SM), Ontological Conflict (OC), and Context Conflict (CC). diagnosis of whether promptable segmentation models distinguish concept-faithful grounding from shortcut-driven responses to misleading attribute cues. To this end, we propose CAFE, the Counterfactual Attribute Factuality Evaluation for promptable segmentation models. CAFE preserves the target region and its annotation mask while counterfactually manipulating attributes that affect concept identity, including surface appearance, surrounding context, and material composition. This design tests whether model responses remain consistent with human semantic judgments when the target region remains localizable but contains misleading attribute cues. We design three categories of attribute-level interventions: superficial mimicry, context conflict, and ontological conflict. Each intervention preserves the target region and its segmentation mask while modifying one attribute dimension that affects concept identity. Superficial mimicry modifies surface appearance to make the target visually resemble another category while preserving its underlying object identity. Context conflict modifies the surrounding context to introduce environmental evidence associated with another category while preserving the target objects identity. Ontological conflict modifies material composition so that the target region changes its substance while preserving its global shape. These interventions create cases where the target remains localizable, but the misleading negative prompt is semantically invalid according to human judgment despite being supported by salient attribute cues. Fig. 1 shows representative examples. These examples demonstrate that promptable segmentation models may produce confident masks for semantically invalid negative prompts when the edited target remains localizable and contains misleading attribute cues. In superficial mimicry, suitcase is painted with giraffe-like patterns while its object identity remains suitcase. The positive prompt is therefore suitcase, whereas the misleading negative prompt is giraffe, which is supported only by the edited surface appearance. In context conflict, teddy bear is placed in snowy scene while its object identity remains teddy bear. The positive prompt remains teddy bear, whereas the misleading negative prompt is polar bear, which is supported by the edited surrounding context rather than the target object itself. In ontological conflict, an airplane-shaped target is re-rendered as cloud while preserving its global shape. The target region is therefore materially cloud rather than an airplane. In this case, the positive prompt is cloud, whereas the misleading negative prompt is real airplane, which is supported only by the retained global shape rather than the material composition of the edited target. We collect source images and annotations from COCO [18], LVIS [6], and SA-Co/Gold [1], and perform controlled attribute-level image editing using category-specific prompts. After multi-stage filtering and validation by three human annotators, CAFE contains 2,146 paired test samples. Each test sample consists of target image, ground-truth mask, positive prompt that describes semantically valid concept, and misleading negative prompt that is visually plausible but semantically invalid for the target region. Our contributions are summarized as follows: i) We introduce CAFE, benchmark for evaluating concept-faithful grounding in promptable segmentation models under controlled counterfactual attribute interventions. CAFE covers three categories of attribute-level semantic conflict, namely superficial mimicry, context conflict, and ontological conflict, which, respectively, manipulate surface appearance, surrounding context, and material composition while preserving the target region and its annotation mask. ii) We construct 2,146 paired test cases, each containing an edited target image, ground-truth mask, positive prompt, and misleading negative prompt. All cases are validated by human annotators to ensure that the target region remains localizable and that the positive and negative prompts reflect clear human semantic judgments under the edited attributes. iii) We evaluate both end-to-end promptable concept segmentation models, such as SAM3, framework-based open-vocabulary grounding-segmentation pipelines, such as Grounded SAM2, and an agentic verification variant that uses SAM3 as segmentation tool, denoted as CAFE-SAM3. The results reveal systematic gap between mask localization quality and concept-faithful grounding: current models can produce accurate masks for misleading negative prompts, indicating that they often respond to salient attribute cues rather than the semantic validity of the queried concept. 2. Related Works Counterfactual Evaluation for Pixel-Level Grounding. Counterfactual evaluation has been widely used to assess whether model predictions rely on causal evidence rather than spurious correlations. Prior work has applied counterfactual or minimally edited inputs to evaluate fairness, robustness, and vision-language understanding [9, 14, 15, 27, 28, 30]. Recent work has begun to examine this issue in segmentation. Generalized referring expression segmentation extends the classical single-target setting to no-target and multi-target expressions, requiring models to decide whether queried concept is visually grounded before producing mask [19]. Counterfactual segmentation benchmarks further diagnose pixel-grounding hallucinations by constructing factual and counterfactual pairs, where models should segment the target in the factual image but abstain when the target object is removed or replaced [17]. Our CAFE follows this counterfactual perspective but studies finer-grained and complementary setting: the target region remains visible and localizable, while attributes such as appearance, material, or context are manipulated. This design tests whether such models faithfully ground the queried concept rather than relying on misleading attribute cues. Open-Vocabulary and Promptable Segmentation. Classical semantic and instance segmentation models are typically trained and evaluated under closed-vocabulary setting, where categories are predefined. SAM [12] and SAM2 [25] relax this paradigm by formulating segmentation as class-agnostic promptable mask prediction, where users provide visual prompts. SAM2 further extends this formulation to video through memory-based promptable segmentation architecture. parallel line of work introduces language into segmentation by combining open-vocabulary detectors or grounding models, such as Grounding DINO [20] and OWLv2 [24]. More recent methods move toward unified open-vocabulary segmentation. YOLO-World [4] improves open-vocabulary detection through vision-language modeling and large-scale region-text pretraining, and extends to instance segmentation with an additional segmentation head. OpenSeeD [37] jointly learns detection and segmentation in shared semantic space. SAM3 [1] further formulates promptable concept segmentation, directly producing masks from concept prompts such as noun phrases, image exemplars, or their combinations. These advances make it increasingly important to evaluate not only whether models can produce accurate masks, but also whether their masks are semantically faithful to the input prompt. Benchmarking Segmentation Models. Segmentation benchmarks have evolved along two axes: output granularity from semantic [21] to instance [7, 8] and panoptic segmentation [11] and interaction paradigmfrom closedvocabulary [8] to visual promptable [13, 25], language-guided or open-vocabulary [26, 33], and promptable concept segmentation [1]. Most benchmarks, such as COCO [18] and LVIS [6], focus on mask overlap metrics (IoU, AP, AR), which only measure spatial accuracy. Some other benchmarks like RefCOCO and RefCOCOg [10, 22, 36] evaluate language-guided localization but do not test whether models reject semantically unsupported or counterfactual queries. SA-Co [1] and HalluSegBench [16] partially address semantic grounding, with HalluSegBench using factual and counterfactual object replacement to reveal pixel-grounding hallucinations. Our CAFE complements these benchmarks by evaluating attribute-level semantic validity under mask-preserving counterfactual edits: the target region remains visible and annotated while appearance or material is manipulated, exposing cases where models produce accurate 4 masks for misleading prompts and revealing shortcut-driven mask retrieval rather than concept-faithful grounding. 3. Task Definition In this section, we formalize the task of evaluating counterfactual attribute factuality for segmentation models. In this work, counterfactual image is defined as an edited version of an original image in which specific attribute of the target region is deliberately changed from its factual state to an alternative state, while the target region remains spatially identifiable and serves as the evaluation anchor. The semantically valid concept after editing may either preserve the original object identity or shift to new materialor substance-defined concept, depending on the type of counterfactual manipulation. This controlled edit introduces visually plausible but semantically invalid competing concept, enabling us to evaluate whether segmentation model follows the semantically valid concept in the edited image or incorrectly responds to the counterfactually induced cue. We define three categories of counterfactual scenarios in which specific visual attributes are manipulated, including superficial patterns, surrounding visual contexts, and substances or materials. 3.1. Counterfactual Attribute Scenarios Superficial Mimicry. The superficial pattern of an object is repainted or covered with confusing pattern associated with another kind of object. For example, as shown in Fig. 2, the vase is recolored with the pattern of watermelon, thereby creating misleading counterfactual cue while keeping the concept of vase semantically valid. The positive prompt therefore refers to the object itself, whereas the misleading negative prompt refers to the repainted superficial pattern. Context Conflict. The visual surroundings of an object are replaced with another environment that is implausible for the object. For example, as shown in Fig. 2, the skateboarder is placed in snowy environment. The positive prompt remains skateboarder, while the misleading prompt is snowboarder, since the person appearing in this scenario is highly plausible as snowboarder. More generally, in context-conflict cases, the positive prompt refers to the original object identity, while the misleading negative prompt refers to contextually plausible but semantically invalid concept suggested by the swapped environment. Ontological Conflict. The substance of the original object is re-rendered and replaced by another kind of material. For example, as shown in Fig. 2, the living dove is re-rendered as crystal sculpture. The positive prompt is therefore amethyst crystal, while the misleading negative prompt is living dove. In general, the positive prompt refers to the re-rendered material or substance, whereas the misleading negative prompt refers to the original object identity that is no longer semantically valid. 3.2. Baseline on CAFE. Non-agentic models maintain relatively details are in Appendix C.2. Table 2 reports cgF , IL_MCC, and pmF 1 highlight that the core high pmF challenge is rejecting semantically invalid concepts, not positive-case segmentation. Grounded SAM2 illustrates this: stable pmF across SM, CC, and OC coexists with consistently low IL_MCC, showing that accurate masks do not 1 guarantee semantic-validity judgments. OC is the most difficult category. Most non-agentic models achieve negative IL_MCC on OC, revealing inverse correlation with semantic labels. Even SAM3, despite an image-level presence head and strong overall performance, drops from 0.857 IL_MCC on CC to -0.241 on OC, suggesting that presence prediction alone cannot resolve ontological counterfactuals. Table 3 analyzes false positives (FPR) and concept swaps. First, non-agentic models exhibit high FPR, frequently accepting misleading prompts. Second, most false positives are target-aligned (IoU > 0.3), except for YOLO-World and Grounded SAM2, indicating that counterfactually edited regions drive errors. Third, OC shows the highest FPR and AFPR across models. In extreme cases, model may accept misleading prompt while rejecting the positive one, reflected in ACSR. Grounded SAM2s low ACSR results from accepting both positive and misleading prompts rather than robust rejection, and must be interpreted alongside its high FPR and AFPR. 1 Overall, these results demonstrate that current open-vocabulary segmentation models struggle to distinguish sculptural or artificial depictions from living entities under ontological conflict, and that positive mask quality does not imply reliable semantic grounding. 5.2. Does Explicit Reasoning Help Counterfactual Segmentation? 1 rises from 38.5 to 63.3, IL_MCC from 0.590 to 0.843, and pmF 1 Leveraging the strong understanding ability of current VLMs for various type of visual content [29, 31, 32], CAFE-SAM3 agent (GPT-5.5) demonstrates the benefit of agentic verification, with details in Appendix D. Compared with direct SAM3, overall cgF from 65.4 to 75.1. The largest gains occur on OC, with cgF increasing from -10.5 to 44.7 and IL_MCC from -0.241 to 0.633, highlighting the utility 1 of explicit reasoning when distinguishing semantically valid concepts from visually plausible but ontologically invalid cues. False-positive also shows that, CAFE-SAM3 reduces overall FPR from 21.2% to 13.5%, AFPR from 20.5% to 11.9%, and ACSR from 8.9% to 1.7% compared with SAM3. Gains are especially pronounced on OC (FPR 66.3% 29.2%, AFPR 65.6%25.8%, ACSR 37.8%6.8%), indicating that agentic verification primarily improves rejection of semantically invalid target-aligned masks rather than positive-case segmentation. OC false-positive rates remain higher 9 than those of SM and CC, suggesting room for improvement in handling ontological counterfactuals. Two additional insights emerge. First, SAM3s image-level presence head enhances robustness on SM and CC, where misleading cues stem from surface appearance or context, but is insufficient for OC. Second, the strong improvement of CAFE-SAM3 agent shows that explicit verification enables segmentation systems to better distinguish semantically invalid concepts from visually plausible counterfactual cues. 6. Conclusion We introduced CAFE, counterfactual attribute factuality evaluation framework for promptable concept segmentation, comprising 2,146 paired samples with positive and misleading prompts across Superficial Mimicry, Context Conflict, and Ontological Conflict. Our results reveal that current open-vocabulary segmentation models often fail to reject semantically invalid concepts under counterfactual cues. SAM3s image-level presence head improves robustness in some cases but remains insufficient for ontological conflicts. CAFE-SAM3 agent demonstrates that MLLM-based reasoning can reduce false positives and concept swaps, suggesting path toward more reliable promptable segmentation. 7. Limitations CAFE currently evaluates single counterfactually edited target per image, allowing controlled assessment of misleading prompts. It does not cover more complex scenes with multiple counterfactual instances or co-occurrence with unedited instances of the same or related concepts. Consequently, counterfactual robustness in crowded or mixed-instance scenarios remains untested.

International Journal of Computer Vision, 127(3):302321, 2019. 12 A. Dataset Preparation A.1. CAFE Annotation Pipeline The CAFE annotation pipeline is shown in Fig. 4. To fit the input resolution of Gemini, we apply affine transformations to the original images and annotations from the validation sets of COCO [18], LVIS [6], and SA-Co/Gold [1]. The transformed annotations are inherited from the source image-annotation pairs, while Gemini-3 is used to generate editing instructions with prompt-engineered inputs containing multiple in-context cases based on the queried instance and the input image. Details of the prompt-engineering cases are provided in Appendix A.2. Based on the generated editing instructions, Nano-banana-2 performs image editing. The annotation format includes the positive prompt, the negative prompt, the corresponding editing type, the editing instruction, and the rationale. The 48,423 raw generated samples are then filtered by human annotators to remove low-quality cases, including poor mask alignment and implausible editing instructions. The filtered images are further reviewed by three human experts. An image is included in the final dataset only when at least two reviewers agree that the edit is reliable and semantically valid, thereby reducing the effect of individual annotator bias. The interface of the annotation frontend is shown in Fig. 5. After this high-selectivity filtering process, 2,146 samples are retained for the final dataset, corresponding to retention rate of 4.4%. Figure 4: Overview of the CAFE dataset annotation pipeline. We draw image-annotation pairs from COCO, SA-Co, and LVIS. The images and annotations are first processed with affine transformations to fit the input size required by Gemini 3, and are then fed into Gemini 3 to generate corresponding editing instructions. Based on the generated instructions, we use nano-banana to perform image editing for all three counterfactual categories. The raw edits then undergo three-stage filtering and cross-checking process. In the first stage, human annotators filter the raw edits and remove images with obvious artifacts. In the second stage, human annotators perform fine-grained review of edit quality and prompt plausibility for both positive and negative prompts. In the third stage, three human editors cross-check all remaining pairs and produce the final high-quality dataset. A.2. Prompts and Models for Dataset Generation A.2.1. Shared Task Head and Output Schema CAFE Auto-Prompt Pipeline Shared Header & Output Schema I. Task Overview. You MUST generate <TARGET_TYPE> counterfactual edit for the <INSTANCE_CATEGORY> instance in the given image. Do not produce any other edit type. If no valid edit of this type exists for this instance, return exactly the literal string None and terminate. II. Output Format. The model must return exactly the following XML-style fields. <type> [The edit type. Must be exactly the target type specified above.] </type> <edit_instruction> [One-sentence, imperative editing directive. Specify exactly what changes and explicitly state what must remain unchanged.] </edit_instruction> <rationales> [One or two sentences explaining the intended conflict. No repetition of the instruction .] </rationales> <positive_prompt> [For Superficial Mimicry and Context Conflict, the positive prompt is the original category of the input instance. For Ontological Conflict, it is the category of the newly rendered instance.] </positive_prompt> <negative_prompt> [For Superficial Mimicry, the negative prompt is the category being mimicked. For Context Conflict, it must be concrete object category plausible in the new context but distinct from the original instance. For Ontological Conflict, it is the category of the original instance. In all cases, the negative prompt must be standard, atomic object category. Context-dependent phrases like "street animal", "snow animal", " beach dog" are strictly prohibited.] </negative_prompt> A.2.2. Prompt for Superficial Mimicry Editing Instruction Generation I. Rules for Superficial Mimicry Surface-Only Change. Modification must affect appearance only (color, pattern, or printed texture). The underlying material, volume, thickness, and physical structure must remain unchanged. The edit must behave like thin visual overlay (paint, print, dye, wrapping), not material transformation. The applied appearance must preserve overall identity recognizability of the object category. The conflicting concept must exist as physical surface appearance, not as depicted image (screen content, 14 photo, poster, logo). Appearances implying volume-level material transformation (solid metal body, glass body, stone sculpture) are not allowed. Textures or patterns without distinctive, widely recognizable category-level signature are not allowed. The applied appearance must introduce visual cues strongly associated with different object category. Global Coverage. The new appearance must uniformly cover the entire visible surface of the instance. All visible parts and components of the object must be consistently modified. Partial edits, localized patches, or unchanged regions are not allowed. Label Exclusivity. The negative prompt must represent different object category, not material variant, subtype, or attribute description of the same object. A.2.3. Prompt for Ontological Conflict Editing Instruction Generation II. Rules for Ontological Conflict Substance Replacement. The object must appear to be entirely made of different substance while its geometry is unchanged. The transformation must change the objects physical nature (living inanimate, organic mineral, functional sculpture). The transformation must imply new object identity (sculpture, statue, mineral object, fluid mass), not material variant of the original object. Complete Transformation. All visible parts must share the new substance. Partial material changes (only surface, only fur, or selected components) are not allowed. Volume-Level Change. The transformation must imply full volumetric material change, not surface coating, paint, or texture overlay. Category Displacement. If the original category is defined by its function (fork, bicycle), the transformation must render the object non-functional (a transparent soap-film fork, stone statue of bicycle). After the transformation the object must be more naturally interpreted as the new category than the original. If global geometry still strongly determines the original category (ice table, wooden bicycle, marble toilet), the edit is invalid. Semantic Exclusivity. If the edited object would still be naturally described as [material] + original category, the edit is invalid. The original category must no longer serve as the primary identity. A.2.4. Prompt for Context Conflict Editing Instruction Generation III. Rules for Context Conflict Foreground Invariance. Target instance pixels must remain identical to the original image. No recoloring, relighting, shadow change, specular change, reflection change, refraction change, blur change, edge halo, or color spill on the instance. No new occluders may overlap the instance. No transparency changes, no see-through effects. 15 Any change inside the instance mask invalidates the sample. Background-Only Edit. Only pixels outside the instance mask may change. Replace the environment with photo-realistic background that matches the original camera viewpoint, perspective, and depth-of-field, but must not alter the instance appearance. Do not add, remove, or modify any foreground objects in way that could plausibly create competing instances of the negative-prompt category. Counterfactual Strength. The new environment must be strongly incompatible with the instances typical context (an implausible placement), not common co-occurrence. If the new environment is natural or frequent context for the object, the sample is invalid. A.2.5. In-context cases for Superficial Mimicry In-Context Cases Superficial Mimicry (A) Positive Cases Case A1. Input instance: elephant. <edit_instruction> Recolor the elephants skin with the pattern of zebra. Keep the elephants pose, shape, and lighting identical; the entire background must be unchanged. <rationales> The elephant is body-painted with zebra-like stripes; large animals can be temporarily painted for exhibition or filming, so the photo is physically plausible while creating surface-only conflict between the zebras iconic striping and the elephants intact anatomy. <Positive Prompt> elephant Case A2. Input instance: chair. <edit_instruction> Overlay the entire chair with continuous coral reef texture of branching pink and <Negative Prompt> zebra white corals, preserving every contour, leg, and seat surface of the chair unchanged. <rationales> Surface decals or spray-painted murals can coat furniture with biological motifs, so coralpatterned chair is realistic installation-art prop while keeping its ontology as chair intact. <Negative Prompt> coral <Positive Prompt> chair Case A3. Input instance: laptop. <edit_instruction> Apply photorealistic ladybug shell texture (red with black polka dots and glossy chitinous finish) uniformly across the laptops case, preserving ports, keyboard, hinge, and all geometric details. <rationales> Laptop skins in printed decal form are commercially available, so ladybug-shell laptop is realistic product staging while producing pure textural conflict. <Negative Prompt> ladybug <Positive Prompt> laptop (B) Negative Cases Case B1. Input instance: potted plant. <edit_instruction> Change the plants leaves and stem texture to resemble metallic wires and circuits, while keeping its silhouette and spatial extent unchanged. Why invalid: replacing leaves and stems with metal circuits implies material-level transformation rather than surface overlay, which crosses into Ontological-Conflict territory (Surface-Only Change violated). <Positive Prompt> potted plant Case B2. Input instance: TV. <edit_instruction> Change the TV screen display to show an aquarium scene with vivid corals and colorful <Negative Prompt> circuits fish, keeping the TVs shape, frame, and positioning identical and the background unchanged. Why invalid: the aquarium exists only as depicted imagery on the screen, not as distinct physical surface, so it is not valid Superficial-Mimicry case (Conflicting concept must be physical surface, not depicted 16 content). <Positive Prompt> TV Case B3. Input instance: bench. <edit_instruction> Apply realistic wooden texture to the bench as surface coating, while keeping its <Negative Prompt> aquarium silhouette, volume, and pose identical. Why invalid: the negative prompt wooden bench is true-positive refinement of the original object, not distinct confounding class (Label Exclusivity violated). <Positive Prompt> bench <Negative Prompt> wooden bench A.2.6. In-context cases for Ontological Conflict In-Context Cases Ontological Conflict (A) Positive Cases Case A1. Input instance: dog. <edit_instruction> Transform the dogs entire body into translucent crystal sculpture; preserve the skeletal pose, muscular silhouette, and fur-like surface micro-geometry through refractive and reflective glass-like properties; keep the background unchanged. <rationales> Life-sized crystal animal sculptures are recognized art form, so converting dog into glass-cast form is physically realizable and establishes new primary identity as sculpture. <Positive Prompt> crystal sculpture Case A2. Input instance: bicycle. <edit_instruction> Transform the bicycle into life-size bronze monument-style statue, preserving every tube, wheel, and handlebar shape, with realistic patinated bronze surface; keep the background identical. <rationales> Bronze monuments of everyday vehicles exist in sculpture parks, so casting bicycle in bronze <Negative Prompt> living dog is realistic; the monument identity supplants the functional bicycle identity. <Positive Prompt> bronze sculpture Case A3. Input instance: cake. <edit_instruction> Transform the cake into fully polished granite monolith, preserving the tiered <Negative Prompt> functional bicycle geometry, edge details, and spatial orientation; keep the background unchanged. <rationales> Granite sculptures of everyday objects are known sculptural trope; the substance change from sponge/cream to granite supplants the cake identity with stone-monument identity. <Positive Prompt> granite sculpture (B) Negative Cases <Negative Prompt> edible cake Case B1. Input instance: cat. <edit_instruction> Transform the cats fur into solid, glossy marble texture while keeping its pose, shape, and spatial extent unchanged. Why invalid: the instruction limits the transformation to fur, leaving other body parts biological partial substance change does not constitute full ontological conflict (Complete Transformation violated). <Positive Prompt> marble sculpture Case B2. Input instance: dining table. <edit_instruction> Transform the dining tables substance into transparent ice while preserving its original <Negative Prompt> living cat shape, dimensions, and spatial orientation. Why invalid: an ice dining table is still functionally dining table, so dining table remains correct label and the transformation fails the label-exclusivity requirement (Category Displacement / Semantic Exclusivity violated). <Positive Prompt> ice sculpture <Negative Prompt> dining table 17 Case B3. Input instance: toilet. <edit_instruction> Transform the toilet into solid marble while preserving its original shape, size, and positioning; keep the background completely unchanged. Why invalid: marble toilets are real luxury product and are still semantically toilets, so the test fails the requirement that the original category be supplanted (Semantic Exclusivity violated). <Positive Prompt> marble sculpture <Negative Prompt> toilet A.2.7. In-context cases for Context Conflict In-Context Cases Context Conflict (A) Positive Cases Case A1. Input instance: polar bear. <edit_instruction> Replace the arctic background with dense, misty bamboo forest. The polar bear instance pixels must remain strictly identical; do not introduce shadows, reflections, occlusions, edge halos, or any pandas/animals. <rationales> The polar bear is placed in pandas iconic bamboo habitat, forcing the model to rely on the bears actual coloration rather than the habitat prior. <Positive Prompt> polar bear Case A2. Input instance: dog. <edit_instruction> Replace the background with an underwater reef scene while preserving the dogs pixels, pose, lighting, and body contours exactly. Do not introduce fish or sharks; the underwater lighting context must not spill onto the dogs body. <Negative Prompt> panda <rationales> land mammal placed underwater tests robustness against marine-mammal priors; without geometric overlap, the model should still recognize the dog from its anatomy. <Positive Prompt> dog Case A3 (vehicle alternative). Input instance: boat. <edit_instruction> Replace the water around the boat with dry desert landscape of rolling dunes, keeping <Negative Prompt> seal the boats hull, sails, and rigging pixels identical to the original image. Do not add any sand vehicles. <rationales> seaworthy boat stranded on dunes tests habitat-driven confusion with sand sleds while the boats geometry remains intact. <Positive Prompt> boat (B) Negative Cases <Negative Prompt> sand sled Case B1. Input instance: cat. <edit_instruction> Replace the bathroom environment with bustling city street, while keeping the cats pixels, pose, and lighting exactly identical. Why invalid: cat on city street is common, natural occurrence, so the context is not counterfactual enough; the negative prompt street animal is also hypernym rather than an atomic category (Counterfactual Strength violated). <Positive Prompt> cat Case B2. Input instance: bed. <edit_instruction> Replace the background with stadium full of cheering fans while keeping the bed <Negative Prompt> street animal and all objects on it unchanged. Why invalid: stadium describes scene/place, not competing foreground object, so it cannot be valid negative prompt for bed (Counterfactual Strength violated). <Positive Prompt> bed Case B3. Input instance: sink. <edit_instruction> Replace the bathroom background with realistic outdoor beach setting, while keeping <Negative Prompt> stadium 18 Figure 5: Data annotation engine used for human quality inspection. Human annotators use the interface to check edit plausibility, mask alignment, and prompt validity during the multi-round filtering process. the sink pixels unchanged. Why invalid: beach equipment is functional collection noun without definable geometry, so it cannot be valid segmentation-level negative prompt (Counterfactual Strength violated). <Positive Prompt> sink <Negative Prompt> beach equipment A.2.8. More Discussions on the Prompts for Ontological Conflict In this section, we discuss how we design the positive and negative prompts for ontological conflicts. We are aware that in earlier benchmarks such as LVIS [6] and SA-Co [1], an instance may correspond to multiple positive categories. For example, LVIS [6] emphasizes the annotation of overlapping categories: toy deer can be annotated as toy, deer, and toy deer. Such ambiguity is acceptable in earlier datasets, since language ambiguity naturally exists in category annotation. However, for counterfactual reasoning and scenarios that require concept-faithful grounding, more exclusive and precise referring expressions are needed. To this end, and to avoid controversial cases, all negative prompts in the ontological conflict category are strictly verified by human expert annotators and cross-checked based on consensus. These negative prompts are constructed with restrictive modifiers to reduce semantic ambiguity, as illustrated in Fig. 6. For example, if the original object is an airplane but the edited target region is re-rendered as cloud with an airplane-like shape, we use real airplane as the negative prompt instead of the standalone prompt airplane. This avoids the ambiguity caused by visual resemblance Figure 6: Illustration of category ambiguity and prompt disambiguation in ontological conflicts. Left: an object may validly belong to multiple categories, such as toy deer belonging to both the toy and deer categories. Right: cloud with an airplane-like shape visually resembles an airplane, but it remains cloud rather than real airplane. Therefore, CAFE uses precise negative prompts such as real airplane instead of ambiguous prompts such as airplane to reduce semantic ambiguity. between the edited cloud region and the original object category. Similarly, when person is re-rendered as sculpture, we use prompts such as living human or real person as the negative prompts to avoid overlap with ambiguous categories. The same principle applies to other objects: if blender is re-rendered as wax sculpture and human consensus determines that the edited target is sculpture, we use functional blender rather than the standalone prompt blender as the negative prompt. This design ensures that the ontological conflict cases can test model hallucination with minimal semantic controversy. All ontological conflict edits are strictly reviewed, and their accepted proportion is therefore lower than that of the other two categories, since the acceptance criteria are intentionally stringent. 20 B. More Examples from CAFE Figure 7: Additional examples from CAFE. Each sample consists of counterfactually edited image, an inherited target mask, semantically valid positive prompt, and visually plausible but semantically invalid misleading prompt. The examples cover Superficial Mimicry (SM), Context Conflict (CC), and Ontological Conflict (OC), demonstrating the diversity of object categories, prompt pairs, and attribute-level counterfactual conflicts in CAFE. 21 C. Implementation Details for Baseline Model Evaluation C.1. Compute Resources. All experiments are evaluation-only inference runs and do not involve model training or fine-tuning. We run the segmentation baselines on NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPUs with 32GB memory. Each model is evaluated on the fixed CAFE benchmark using the same image-prompt pairs and evaluation scripts. The total compute cost is dominated by model inference over the benchmark and threshold calibration for models without native presence head. The agentic CAFE-SAM3 diagnostic probe additionally requires calls to the MLLM verifier, but does not require gradient-based optimization. C.2. Version of Models Adopted YOLO-World. We use YOLO-World-Seg-L [4], specifically the seg-head-finetuned checkpoint released in the official repository, which preserves the open-vocabulary detection ability of the base YOLO-World detector while adding instance segmentation. SAM3. We use the official SAM 3 [1] checkpoint released by Meta on Hugging Face. OpenSeeD. We use the official OpenSeeD [37] release with the Swin-T backbone, trained on COCO panoptic segmentation and Objects365. Grounded SAM2. We use the official grounding-dino-base checkpoint from Grounding DINO [20] for textconditioned object grounding, and apply SAM 2.1 with the Hiera-Large checkpoint as the segmentation model. OWLv2 + SAM. We use the OWLv2 [5] google/owlv2-large-patch14-ensemble checkpoint from Hugging Face for open-vocabulary object detection, followed by Segment Anything (SAM) [13] with the ViT-H checkpoint, facebook/sam-vit-huge, for mask prediction. CAFE-SAM3 Agent. We evaluate an agentic pipeline that uses SAM 3 as segmentation tool. The MLLM agent interacts with SAM 3 through four tool calls, segment_phrase, examine_masks, select_masks_and_return, and report_no_mask, for up to 10 turns per episode. SAM 3 is loaded locally with confidence threshold of 0.5, and each segment_phrase call runs SAM 3 with the queried text prompt. For each CAFE target, we run the agent separately with the positive and negative prompts on the edited image. We evaluate the resulting masks using the same segm cgF protocol as the other baselines. For the details about the CAFE-SAM3 system prompt please refer to 1 Appendix D. C.3. Calibration on Confidence Threshold For earlier open-vocabulary detectors that lack dedicated presence-confidence head, we calibrate the detection threshold following the baseline calibration protocol in Sec. F.1 of the SAM 3 paper [1]. Specifically, SAM 3 [1] calibrates OWLv2 [5], GroundingDINO [20] by sweeping the detection threshold at intervals of 0.1 and selecting the threshold that maximizes LVIS cgF on the box detection task. The selected threshold is then applied to the remaining 1 datasets for both box detection and instance segmentation evaluation. Following the SAM 3 [1] baseline calibration protocol, we calibrate the score threshold for each baseline on the LVIS-based box detection task. In our implementation, we sweep the threshold from 0.05 to 0.95 with step size of 0.05 and select the value that maximizes LVIS cgF . The selected threshold is then fixed for CAFE evaluation. We use the threshold of 0.2 for OWLv2 [5], 0.2 for Grounded SAM2 [26], 0.15 for OpenSeeD [37], and 0.15 for Yolo-World [4]. 1 C.4. Threshold Sensibility of Target-aligned Metrics We use τ = 0.3 throughout. Fig. 8 shows that AFPR and ACSR remain stable when τ varies from 0.3 to 0.7 across all subsets. For example, OC-AFPR changes by less than 0.025. This stability indicates that most target-aligned false positives have high overlap with the annotated target region, rather than being caused by marginal or imprecise mask alignment. When τ is increased to 0.9, some predictions are reclassified from TA-FP to UA-FP, but the overall 22 Figure 8: IoU-threshold sensitivity of AFPR and ACSR on CAFEval2026. Both metrics are computed for SAM3 at fixed score threshold = 0.5 and swept over τ [0.3, 0.9] with step 0.1. Curves are flat for τ [0.3, 0.7] across every subset, indicating that the models wrong predictions overlap the source target with high IoU ( 0.7); the failures are therefore semantic-grounding errors, not boundary-precision errors. The drop at τ = 0.9 reflects re-classification of high-IoU errors from TA-FP to UA-FP (the image-level rate IL-FPR is preserved), supporting our use of τ = 0.3 as the canonical operating point. The dotted vertical line marks τ = 0.3. image-level false positive rate remains unchanged. We therefore use τ = 0.3 as the lowest threshold that still captures meaningful target alignment. 23 D. SAM3-CAFE Agent D.1. System Prompt SAM3-CAFE Agent System Prompt (Multi-turn with Tool Calls) You are an expert segmentation agent that processes human prompt inputs and images. Given an image and concept prompt, your task is to determine whether the queried concept genuinely exists in the image based on visual evidence, and if so, produce segmentation masks that accurately cover it. You operate inside an interactive loop with access to segmentation tool (SAM3). Each turn, you analyze the current evidence, reason about the concept, and call exactly one tool. You then receive the tool result and continue reasoning in the next turn. You have maximum of 10 turns. Each tool call consumes one turn. After each tool result, you will be told the current turn number and how many turns remain. You must call segment_phrase at least once before making any final decision (select_masks_and_return or report_no_mask). Always let SAM3 attempt segmentation first rather than deciding purely from the image alone. Before calling select_masks_and_return, you must call examine_masks at least once to zoom in and inspect the mask region(s) you intend to accept. This is required even if the masks appear correct at first glance, because visual details such as material, texture, and surface properties are often only distinguishable at close range. Conversely, if SAM3 did not return any masks, that does not necessarily mean the concept is absent from the image. It may be due to an unsuitable prompt or limitation of SAM3s vocabulary. Consider trying more general or alternative noun phrase before concluding with report_no_mask. Your conversation history is fully preserved across turns. You can refer back to previous tool results, your earlier reasoning, and any images you have seen. The original image is provided once at the beginning and remains in your context throughout. Exit behavior: If you call select_masks_and_return, the selected masks are output as the final segmentation result and the session ends. If you call report_no_mask, the session ends with no segmentation output. If you reach the maximum turn limit (10 turns) without calling either select_masks_and_return or report_no_mask, the session ends automatically and the most recent SAM3 segmentation output is used as the final result. Environment and Execution Flow Turn 1: You receive the original image and concept prompt (e.g., "Help me segment bicycle in this image."). You analyze the image, reason about the prompt, and call segment_phrase with an appropriate noun phrase. Subsequent turns: You receive the tool result: text description and possibly an image showing numbered masks or zoom-in crops. The message also indicates your current turn number and remaining turns. You analyze the result, compare against the original image and prompt, and decide your next action: Call segment_phrase again with different noun phrase if the previous attempt was unsuccessful. Call examine_masks to zoom into specific mask regions for closer inspection of material, texture, or fine details. Call select_masks_and_return to accept masks and finish. Call report_no_mask if you determine the concept does not exist in the image. The loop ends when you call select_masks_and_return, report_no_mask, or when you exhaust all 10 turns. Visual Reasoning Guidance Images may contain objects whose visual attributes have been modified. For example, an objects surface texture may have been changed, its surrounding context may have been altered, or its material composition may have been replaced while its shape is preserved. These modifications are realistic enough to be visually plausible but may cause the depicted object to no longer match certain concept labels. When evaluating whether concept is present: Do not rely solely on shape or silhouette, e.g., statue shaped like person but made of marble is sculpture, not person. Do not rely solely on surrounding context, e.g., toy placed in kitchen is still toy, not kitchen utensil. Do not rely solely on surface pattern, e.g., car wrapped in camouflage vinyl is still car, not military vehicle. Focus on the actual material, substance, and physical properties visible in the image. If you are uncertain, use examine_masks to zoom in and inspect texture details before making judgment. When evaluating concept, look beyond any single visual cue. Consider the objects physical properties, material composition, 24 and how it appears within the broader scene. concept is present only if the object in the image would be naturally and correctly described by that concept by careful human observer, taking into account both the objects intrinsic properties and its relationship with the surrounding scene. For example, when judging material-specific concepts, consider physical properties such as transparency, reflectivity, surface roughness, and texture. Glass tends to be transparent and refractive, metal tends to be specular and reflective, stone tends to be opaque, ice tends to be translucent, and so on. However, these are only salient indicators. Materials can exhibit range of visual characteristics depending on lighting, finish, and condition, so examine the full set of visible cues in context rather than relying on any single property. If the visible surface properties contradict the queried material, the concept may not be present. Understanding the User Query 1. If there are multiple instances of the target object class in the image, read the query carefully to determine whether it applies to all instances or just one, and ground accordingly. 2. Identify the actual target object the user is asking you to ground. Do not ground secondary objects that only exist to help identify the target. For example, given "a giraffe with its head up", ground the whole giraffe, not just the head. Given "a person holding blender with their left hand", ground the person, not the blender or hand. 3. Do not include masks for objects mentioned only for identification purposes. For example, given "a man carrying young girl", ground only the man. 4. Sometimes the target is not directly named but clearly referenced. For example, given "something that shows the man is playing golf" and an image of man holding golf club, ground the golf club. 5. Carefully examine all details in the image and reason step by step. Do not give up and call report_no_mask due to small technicalities. Only call report_no_mask if there are clear, direct contradictions between the query and the image content. 6. If the query contains typos, grammatical errors, or irrelevant information, reason about the users intent based on the image content rather than following the query literally. Available Tools You must call exactly one tool per turn. Enclose the tool call in <tool> ... </tool> tags. segment_phrase Use SAM3 to segment all instances of simple noun phrase in the image. Returns an image with numbered, color-coded masks overlaid on the original image, along with the number of masks found. Parameters: {"text_prompt": Returns: An image with numbered masks overlaid, and text message indicating how many masks were found. If no masks are found, only text message is returned indicating 0 masks, and you should try different text_prompt. Rules for segment_phrase: "a short, simple noun phrase"} 1. Use simple, direct noun phrases. You may include visual adjectives like color (e.g., "brown dog", "red car"), but avoid complex descriptors, numbers, actions, relationships, or comparatives. 2. Do not try to ground text, letters, or numbers written on objects. Use the object category instead (e.g., "sign" instead of the text on the sign). 3. If phrase produces no masks or incomplete results, try more general noun phrase. For example, if "elementary school teacher" returns nothing, try "person". 4. Avoid identifying concepts through actions or relationships. Use "vase" instead of "the bigger vase", "dog" instead of "the dog lying down". 5. If results are not what you expected, try different text_prompt. Be creative with synonyms and visual common sense. 6. For niche objects that produce no masks, try grounding more general category. For example, if "sundial" fails, try "statue". 7. Keep your text_prompt concise. Do not make it long. 8. Never use the exact same text_prompt more than once. 9. When grounding person, use general phrases like "person", "man", "girl" that refer to the whole person. Do not ground identifying parts or attributes (e.g., do not use "white hat" to find guy with white hat). 10. If previous text_prompt did not work, think of new, creative phrase. For example, when grounding the center of cake with text, try "birthday greeting". 25 11. Always call segment_phrase with text_prompt that represents the entire grounding target. Do not use subparts (e.g., use "adult person" not "adult hand"). 12. If the query refers to one specific instance among several, use the singular category name and then use select_masks_and_return to pick the correct one. 13. Every call to segment_phrase generates fresh set of masks. Previous masks are no longer rendered on the latest image, though they remain visible in earlier images in your conversation history. 14. Only ground objects that fully match the query. Ignore partial matches. 15. Do not propose text_prompt that covers more area than the query asks for (e.g., do not use "jeans" when asked for broken areas of jeans). 16. Do not propose text_prompt that covers less area than the query asks for (e.g., do not use "microphone" when asked for the person holding microphone). 17. Try to propose text_prompt that covers exactly the queried object(s), no more and no less. 18. Be creative in your text_prompt choices. Use synonyms and visual common sense. You have multiple turns, so take your time. examine_masks Zoom into specific mask regions for close-up inspection. Returns high-resolution cropped images of the requested mask areas with minimal overlay, preserving material and texture details. Use this when you need to verify fine-grained visual properties such as material, texture, surface quality, or small details that are hard to judge from the full image. Parameters: {"mask_indices": Rules for examine_masks: [1, 3]} 1. You may only call examine_masks after segment_phrase has produced masks. 2. mask_indices must be non-empty array of valid mask numbers (1 to N, where is the number of masks in the most recent segment_phrase result). Out-of-range indices will be ignored. 3. Use this tool when you need to inspect material, texture, or fine details to determine whether the mask region truly matches the queried concept. 4. The returned zoom-in images do not have mask number labels to avoid occluding details. The images are returned in the order you requested, with text description indicating which mask each image corresponds to. 5. You do not need to examine every mask. Only examine the ones where you are uncertain about the concept match. select_masks_and_return Select subset of (or all) masks from the most recent segment_phrase result as your final answer. This ends the conversation. Parameters: {"final_answer_masks": Rules for select_masks_and_return: [1, 2]} 1. Only call this when you are confident the selected masks correctly cover the queried concept. 2. Mask numbers refer to the most recent segment_phrase result image. Do not reference masks from earlier calls. 3. The integers in final_answer_masks must be within range 1 to (number of masks in the most recent image), with no duplicates. 4. The selected masks should accurately capture the target object(s) and only the target object(s). 5. Before calling this tool, verify that each selected mask matches the original user query (not just the intermediate text_prompt you used for segment_phrase). 6. If the query involves colors, double-check against the original image since mask overlays change object colors. 7. If the query involves relative positions, explicitly reason about each masks spatial position before selecting. report_no_mask Report that the queried concept does not exist in the image. This ends the conversation. Parameters: {} (empty object) Rules for report_no_mask: 1. Only call this when you have carefully examined the image and determined that no object matches the queried concept. 2. If at any point in your reasoning you identified matching target, you must not call report_no_mask. Use select_masks_and_return instead. 3. Before calling this tool, re-examine the original image and explicitly restate why no object matches the query. 4. Be thorough: if the query is slightly inaccurate but related object exists, ground that object instead of reporting no mask. 26 5. Do not call report_no_mask due to minor discrepancies. Only use it when there is clear, fundamental mismatch between the query and the image content.
